Planet surface — one of several possible worlds, all randon seededIt started with a simple wish. My 10-year-old son, Justus, wanted to travel to other planets, build spaceships out of blocks, and blow up asteroids.
As a CIO with 25 years of software development experience, I knew what building a 3D multiplayer voxel game from scratch usually entails: months of coding, asset creation, and wrestling with network protocols. But I also wanted to see exactly where the boundaries of Generative AI currently lie. So, under our new family label “JuMaVe Games” (Justus, Marcel, and Verena), we decided to find out.
The rule was simple: I wouldn’t write a single line of code myself.
The result is Blocks Beyond The Stars — a fully playable 3D multiplayer game built with Unity 6 and a .NET 8 dedicated server. One hundred percent of the code, 3D models, textures, and music were generated by an AI ensemble.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

StartscreenOur first Short on YouTube (cutted by Claude):
https://www.youtube.com/shorts/jGS-yNJveYc
Here is how we did it, and the hard lessons we learned about the reality of AI in software development.
The Team: The Visionary and the Architect
We quickly fell back into the successful workflow we established during our previous hardware projects. Justus was the Product Owner and Lead Game Designer. He dictated the rules of the universe: how the voxel building should feel, how the spaceships should move, and what the game loop looked like.
My role was the Technical Wingman and Architect. Instead of writing C# scripts, I orchestrated a suite of AI tools:

• Claude: Coding, Testing, CI/CD Pipeline, Video-Cut

• ChatGPT: Image generation and Texture generation. Research, Brainstorming, Planning.

• Gemini: Story-Planning, In-Game Story writing.

• ElevenLabs: Soundeffects.

• Suno.ai: Composed the atmospheric space soundtrack.
The Technical Reality: What AI Can (and Can’t) Do
I have seen a lot of AI hype, but building a networked 3D game exposed the raw truth about where AI shines and where it completely breaks down.
1. The Architecture Trap If you tell an AI, “Make me a 3D space game,” it will spit out a quick, dirty, unscalable single-player prototype. Left to its own devices, AI generates massive technical debt. To make this work, I had to force a strict Client/Server architecture from day one. The server runs purely on .NET 8 handling the UDP network code and game state, while the Unity 6 client only handles rendering and input. The AI was a brilliant tradesman, but I had to be the uncompromising architect. Without those guardrails, the codebase would have collapsed into spaghetti code within hours.
2. DevOps in the Kids’ Room Because AI generates code so rapidly, the bottleneck is no longer writing syntax — it’s deployment. To keep up with Justus’s feature requests, we had to build a fully automated CI/CD pipeline. The AI writes the code, the pipeline builds the game, updates the project website, and pushes the latest release directly to itch.io. Automating the release process was the only way we had the bandwidth to actually playtest and balance the game together.
3. The 80/20 Reality Check AI is a miracle worker for the first 80%. It built the voxel engine and generated assets at a blistering pace. But the remaining 20% — handling edge cases, network interpolation, security, and game balancing — took the absolute lion’s share of the effort.
The Architect vs. The Builder
Building Blocks Beyond The Stars answered a question that is currently keeping the tech industry awake at night: Does AI make software developers obsolete?
My answer is a resounding no. In fact, we need them more than ever.
AI removes the friction of syntax and boilerplate. It empowers a 10-year-old to see his imagination rendered on a screen in real-time. But a Proof of Concept is not a product. Transforming an AI-generated prototype into robust, production-ready software still requires human professionals who understand system architecture, security, and long-term maintainability. We are simply outsourcing the grunt work to the machine so human intelligence can focus on quality and innovation.
You can explore the universe Justus dreamed up, or dive into the AI-generated architecture yourself:
💻 GitHub (Open Source): Take a look under the hood at the C#/.NET 8 architecture BlocksBeyondTheStars Repository
🚀 Project Website: blocksbeyondthestars.com
🕹️ Play the Beta: jumavegames.itch.io/blocks-beyond-the-stars
